speaker: interviewer
text: And tape number 6. Rena, is there anything else that you would like to say to us today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to warn people about the deniers of the Holocaust. There are so many deniers of the Holocaust, some of them publish important sounding papers, or papers that seem to have a lot of details, and of course, they are all lies. And that's, of course, another reason why the survivors of the Holocaust feel so compelled to tell their story, share their experiences again and again and again, to try and reach as many people as we can, especially young people, who will have a chance to go out in the world and, perhaps, be able to fight against those who try to deny that the Holocaust happened. Because I can't imagine of a greater tragedy than to have somebody say and somebody believe that Holocaust didn't happen. That would be like killing us all over again. Holocaust did happen, and we have to teach people about it so it won't happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Rena.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my grandmother, my grandmother Sarah Ferber and a picture of me. I was probably about 18 months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that would be 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably at the photographer's. I got a lot of old pictures when I went back to the ghetto, to Krakow, in 1945, and I went to my grandparents' house, where they lived during the ghetto. They lived in a, like, low-- it was, like, on the one-- one floor. And I went into the attic, and I found the pictures scattered in the sawdust. That's how I have all my old pictures. Some of the pictures I got from my aunt and my uncles who survived the war, but the pictures, most of them came from the attic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my mother, my mother, Roza We called her Roza. Roza Windisch Ferber [INAUDIBLE]. And this picture was taken in November, 1962. Actually, it's made from a picture in which there were more people around her. But when my mother died, I really didn't have any good picture of her, so I have taken this small snapshot and given it to a photographer, and this is what he-- he did from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- I think that we lived here already, in this house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Framingham?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my father, [INAUDIBLE] Ferber. This picture was taken, probably, 1938, someplace in-- on vacation, probably, when we were in the country. My father was a very, very handsome man, and I often used to think that Schindler reminded me of my father. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is our ID card. This is what we were given by the American government in Linz, Austria. And this is what we used like a passport to travel through one zone to another. You know, Austria was occupied Germany by four allies, so it is in English, in French, and in Russian. And we could travel all over just by showing it, and we didn't have to pay for a ticket in the beginning. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is the story about Mark and me when we came to the United States. This is from Friday, November 26, which is probably about 10 days after we arrived, or just a week after we arrived. And sitting from left to right is Mark, my girlfriend Sonia. I am sitting right next to Sonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's Sonia's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia Schreiber-Weitz. Next to me is Sonia's sister, Blanca, Blanca Schreiber-Borel. And next to Blanca is her husband, Norbert Borel. And standing behind us is uncle-- Norbert's uncle Harry White, who sent us the affidavit and who helped us an awful lot when we first came, his daughter and son-in-law. His daughter, Sadie-- Sadie Lot and her husband Charlie, who have since passed away-- took us under their wing. Took us to our first Chinese restaurant. Took us to our first movie in the United States. Took us for our first lobster. Became lifelong friends. We spent a lot of time together and played cards, Canasta, together, and we miss them. They were wonderful to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is Anita in July of 1999 with Amy. Amy was just five, five and a half years old, and it was one of the last pictures I have of Anita, before she got so very ill again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken? Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- in my-- in our house in July. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is our granddaughter, the only granddaughter among five boys. And this picture was taken when Amy was 11, last year, in-- probably in September 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in school in-- up in Nyack.

speaker: interviewer
text: New York. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Bert Rappaport, Amy's father, our son-in-law, and his wife, Amy's stepmother, Terry.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken, actually, this last August, August 18, 1996, at a party for one of our cousin's 50th birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wellesly. Wellesly, Massachusetts. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Zachary Bowers our oldest grandson. He's 20. His mother is Marilyn. And this was taken at his graduation in Duchesne, Utah in-- well, about two years ago, which would make it 1994. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And this is Adam Roman Bowers. This our number two grandson. He's 18 years old. He's now a freshman at the State University of Utah. And this picture was taken at his high school graduation, probably 1995, beginning of 1995 in Duchesne, Utah. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Here is Mark, holding our youngest grandson, Joseph Sandperl, Marilyn's youngest boy. And this was-- Joseph was just a little tyke, probably just maybe nine months old. That would be-- make him 1991, in our house, actually, here in Framingham. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] In December 1993, we were very fortunate that we had all our grandchildren together. So of course, we had this photo taken. And standing on the left is Adam. Next to Adam is his brother Zach. Next to Zach, of course, it's Amy. And Joseph was just a month old at that time, in December, 1993. Sitting on Grandpa's lap is David, and next to David is Jason, cuddling to Grandpa. And Mark is sitting among his grandchildren, and I'm sitting next to Mark, next to all my grandchildren. And this was taken in Framingham. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture of David, David Katz, and a picture of Jason Katz. Which one is which? David is on the left, and Jason is on the right. And this picture was taken in-- last year, in 1995. And those are the children of Debbi, Debbi and Abby.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd like you to introduce the part of your family that are with us today. Well, my husband Mark, our daughter Debbi, our youngest daughter Debbi, our baby, and her two little boys. This is Jason, and this is David. Jason is nine, and David is six. Jason is in fourth grade, and David is in kindergarten this year. Debbi what was it like growing up as a child of survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being the youngest of three children, I feel that I always knew, and it was just part of being part of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you feel about your parents giving their testimony today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm very proud. And my mom-- we have recordings and tapings of her speaking. But my dad, I heard his story once when I was young, and my husband and I are very anxious to have his story on tape, to have and to show our children when they get older. They know my mother's story, but they don't know much of my father's story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mark, how do you feel now that you've given your testimony, and Rena has given hers and your family's here with you, or part of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We gave our testimony today, and I think that our testimony should be a lesson [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] against hatred and prejudice in the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jason, what would you like to tell us today? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How do you feel about your grandparents telling their story to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's great that they're teaching other people and future generations about what happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Would anybody like to say anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very happy that-- that Mark gave his testimony today, and I'm very happy that Debbi and the children were able to come, because the rest of the family is just in pictures. So I'm glad it's-- I'm glad we did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
